title: Zabaglione
subtitle: Egg yolks, sugar, and Marsala wine whisked over heat until thick and foamy
excerpt: Egg yolks, caster sugar, and sweet Marsala wine whisked together in a bowl set over barely simmering water for about ten minutes until the mixture triples in volume and drops in thick ribbons from the whisk. Served immediately in glasses, warm, with biscotti or amaretti.
sourceNotes: Zabaglione is an Italian dessert with roots in the cooking of the Piedmont and Emilia-Romagna regions from at least the seventeenth century. The use of Marsala wine became standard in the nineteenth century. It is a staple of Italian domestic and restaurant cooking and has been widely made in Britain since Italian restaurant cooking became established in the twentieth century.

content.text: Zabaglione is one of the few desserts that is made in front of the people who will eat it. It takes about ten minutes of continuous whisking over a bain-marie, and the result must be served immediately, it begins to collapse within a few minutes as it cools. The theatrical element is real rather than contrived: the mixture visibly triples in volume, turns pale, and forms glossy ribbons as the proteins in the yolk cook and trap the air.

Keep the water beneath the bowl at a gentle simmer throughout, not a boil. If the water boils hard, the yolks will scramble before they have time to aerate. Use sweet Marsala (dolce) rather than dry (secco), the dry version is correct for savoury dishes but too sharp in a dessert. The consistency to aim for is thick enough to drop in a ribbon that holds its shape on the surface for a few seconds before dissolving.
A large heatproof bowl that will sit over a saucepan without touching the water. You will also need warmed serving glasses, cold glasses cool the zabaglione too quickly and it collapses faster.
Fill a medium saucepan with about 5 cm of water and bring to a gentle simmer. Set a large heatproof bowl on top, checking that the base of the bowl does not touch the water.

Put 4 egg yolks, {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, and {{fortified-marsala}} of sweet Marsala into the bowl. Whisk with a hand mixer or balloon whisk, keeping the whisk moving constantly, for 8, 12 minutes. The mixture will foam up immediately, then gradually thicken and turn pale as it cooks. It is ready when it has tripled in volume, is very pale yellow, and falls in thick ribbons that hold on the surface for a few seconds. The mixture should reach 75°C if you have a thermometer.
Pour immediately into warmed glasses. Serve with biscotti, amaretti biscuits, or fresh berries. The zabaglione must be eaten within a few minutes of making, it will collapse and sink as it cools.

items.fix: Reduce the heat to a gentle simmer. If you see wisps of steam escaping around the bowl edges but the water is not visibly boiling hard, the temperature is right. Remove from the heat briefly if it is getting too hot.
items.cause: Water below the bowl is boiling too hard
items.symptom: Zabaglione is scrambling and becoming grainy

items.fix: Increase the heat slightly under the saucepan. The bowl base should feel very warm to the touch. Measure the Marsala carefully, too much liquid prevents thickening.
items.cause: Water temperature is too low, or too much Marsala was used
items.symptom: Mixture is not thickening after 12 minutes

items.fix: Warm the serving glasses with hot water before use. Make sure the zabaglione reaches the ribbon stage before pouring.
items.cause: Cold glasses, or mixture wasn't thick enough before being poured

items.fix: Use a bowl that fits snugly over the saucepan. A very wide bowl disperses the heat and the mixture doesn't get hot enough to cook the yolks.
items.cause: Bowl is too large and the heat isn't reaching the mixture effectively
items.symptom: Mixture is frothy but not thickening

content.text: Zabaglione is one of the oldest written-down Italian desserts. Versions show up in Italian cooking from the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. The mix of egg yolks, sugar, and wine whisked over heat appears under many names across Italy. The spelling and the wine vary by region. The Marsala version became standard once the Sicilian fortified wine was easy to get across Italy. In Britain, the dish came in with Italian restaurants and has stayed on menus ever since.
